docs.title: ScoutKeeper Documentation
docs.text: Welcome to the ScoutKeeper user documentation. ScoutKeeper is a free, open-source membership management system for Scout organisations of any size.

docs.title: What is ScoutKeeper?
docs.text: ScoutKeeper is a web-based platform that replaces paper records and spreadsheets with a proper membership system. It runs on standard Linux shared hosting and requires no specialist technical knowledge to operate.

docs.title: Where to start
docs.text:
• New installation? Start with Requirements then follow the Installation guide.
  • Already installed? Jump to First Login to get oriented.
  • Looking for something specific? Use the search bar above.

docs.title: Modules
docs.text:
Module What it does Members Member records, custom fields, waiting list, bulk import Organisation Structure Groups, sections, teams, and hierarchy Events Calendar, event management, iCal feed Achievements Badge and training definitions and awards Communications Articles and email to members Directory Organogram and contact directory Administration Roles, settings, users, backup

Note

ScoutKeeper is under active development. Documentation is updated alongside each release.

docs.title: From a member's record
docs.text:
1. Open the member record
  2. Go to the Achievements tab
  3. Click Award Achievement
  4. Select the achievement type and enter the date awarded
  5. Add a note if relevant
  6. Save

docs.title: Bulk awarding
docs.text:
To award the same achievement to multiple members at once:

  1. Go to Achievements → Bulk Award
  2. Select the achievement type
  3. Search for and select the members
  4. Set the award date and save

docs.title: Viewing awards
docs.text: A member's full achievement history is shown on the Achievements tab of their member record.

docs.title: Defining Achievements
docs.text: Achievement definitions must be created before they can be awarded to members.

docs.title: Creating a definition
docs.text:
1. Go to Achievements → Manage Definitions
  2. Click Add Achievement
  3. Enter:
    • Name — e.g. "Wood Badge"
    • Category — e.g. "Leadership Training"
    • Description (optional)
  4. Save

docs.title: Editing and deactivating
docs.text: Open an existing definition to edit its details. Definitions that are no longer in use can be deactivated — they will no longer appear in the award form but existing awards are retained.

docs.title: Achievements Overview
docs.text: The Achievements module tracks badges, awards, and training records for your members.

docs.title: Concepts
docs.text:
Achievement definitions are the types of awards available in your organisation — e.g. "Scout Badge", "First Aid Training", "Wood Badge".

Awards are instances of an achievement granted to a specific member on a specific date.

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
1. An administrator creates achievement definitions (done once per badge/award type)
  2. Leaders assign those achievements to individual members as they are earned

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Defining Achievements
  • Awarding Achievements

docs.title: Audit Log
docs.text: The audit log records all significant actions taken in the system — who did what and when.

docs.title: Viewing the log
docs.text:
Go to Admin → Audit Log. You can filter by:

  • Date range
  • User
  • Action type (create, update, delete)
  • Record type (member, event, etc.)

docs.title: What is logged
docs.text:
• Member record created, edited, or deleted
  • User login and logout
  • Role assignments changed
  • Settings changed
  • Backup created or downloaded

docs.title: Retention
docs.text: Audit log entries are retained indefinitely and cannot be deleted from the UI.

docs.title: Backups
docs.text: Go to Admin → Backup to create a database backup. The backup contains all member data, settings, and configuration in a compressed SQL dump.

docs.title: Scheduling backups
docs.text:
ScoutKeeper can run automatic backups via the cron job. Configure the backup frequency in Admin → Settings → Backup. Backups are stored in /data/backups/ on your server.

Warning

Backups are stored on the same server as your live data. For proper disaster recovery, download backups regularly and store them off-site.

docs.title: Downloading a backup
docs.text: Click Download next to any backup in the list to download it to your computer.

docs.title: Restoring a backup
docs.text: Restoring requires direct database access. Import the SQL file using phpMyAdmin or the MySQL CLI on your server.

docs.title: Data export
docs.text: Go to Admin → Export to export member data as CSV. You can filter by section before exporting.

docs.title: Language Management
docs.text: ScoutKeeper supports multiple languages. The interface language is selected per-user from the account menu.

docs.title: Available languages
docs.text: The built-in languages shipped with ScoutKeeper are shown in Admin → Language Management along with their completion percentage.

docs.title: Changing the system default language
docs.text: Go to Admin → Settings and change the Default Language. New users will see this language until they change their own preference.

docs.title: Editing translations
docs.text:
Administrators can edit translation strings directly in Admin → Language Management. Select a language, then search for or browse strings to update.

Note

Changes made in the language editor are applied immediately and are not overwritten by system updates. Built-in translations are updated with each release.

docs.title: Adding a new language
docs.text: Contributing a new translation: translations are maintained in the ScoutKeeper GitHub repository. See the Contributing guide for instructions.

docs.title: Roles & Permissions
docs.text: ScoutKeeper uses a role-based access control system. Each user is assigned one or more roles, and each role grants specific permissions.

docs.title: Default roles
docs.text:
ScoutKeeper ships with a set of default roles:

Role Access Administrator Full access to all features Leader Members, Events, Achievements (own section) Secretary Members, Communications (all sections) Read Only View-only access to members and events

docs.title: Creating a role
docs.text:
1. Go to Admin → Roles & Permissions
  2. Click Add Role
  3. Enter a name and select the permissions to grant
  4. Save

docs.title: Assigning roles
docs.text: Roles are assigned to user accounts, not member records. Go to a user's account in Admin → Users & Logins to assign a role.

docs.title: Scoped access
docs.text: Roles can be scoped to a specific section or node. A leader scoped to "1st Scouts Cubs" can only see and edit members in that section.

docs.title: Settings
docs.text: System-wide settings are in Admin → Settings.

docs.title: Organisation
docs.text:
• Organisation name — shown in the header and emails
  • Country — used for date and address formatting
  • Logo — upload your organisation's logo

docs.title: Email
docs.text:
• SMTP host, port, username, password — for outgoing email
  • From address and From name — shown on outgoing emails

docs.title: Registration
docs.text:
• Public registration — enable/disable the public waiting list form
  • Registration sections — which sections appear in the registration form

docs.title: Custom fields
docs.text: Add and manage custom member fields. See Custom Fields.

docs.title: Terms & Conditions
docs.text: Upload or paste the T&C text that members must accept during registration.

docs.title: Notices
docs.text: Create system-wide notices shown to all logged-in users (e.g. "System maintenance on Friday").

docs.title: Users & Logins
docs.text: User accounts are separate from member records. A person can be a member without having a login, and a login account can be created for any existing member.

docs.title: Creating a login
docs.text:
1. Go to Admin → Users & Logins
  2. Click Add User
  3. Search for an existing member record to link the account to
  4. Enter an email address and temporary password
  5. Assign a role
  6. Save — the user can log in immediately

docs.title: Editing a user
docs.text: Open the user to change their role, reset their password, or disable their account.

docs.title: Disabling a user
docs.text: Disabling a user prevents login without deleting the account. The member record is not affected.

docs.title: Multi-factor authentication
docs.text: Users can enable MFA from their own account settings page. Administrators cannot force MFA on other accounts at this time — this is planned for a future release.

docs.title: Articles
docs.text: Articles are posts that appear in the internal news/notice board visible to logged-in users.

docs.title: Creating an article
docs.text:
1. Go to Communications → Articles → New Article
  2. Enter a title and body content
  3. Set the publication date (defaults to now)
  4. Choose visibility (all members, or specific sections)
  5. Save as Draft or Publish immediately

docs.title: Managing articles
docs.text:
The articles list shows all articles with their status. Published articles are visible to members; drafts are only visible to staff.

To unpublish an article, open it and change its status back to Draft.

docs.title: Email
docs.text: The Communications module can send emails to members or subsets of your membership.

docs.title: Composing an email
docs.text:
1. Go to Communications → Email → Compose
  2. Choose recipients — all members, a section, or a custom selection
  3. Enter a subject and body
  4. Click Send or Schedule for later delivery

docs.title: Email queue
docs.text: Emails are sent via a background queue processed by the cron job. Go to Communications → Email → Queue to see pending, sent, and failed messages.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
Outgoing email requires SMTP to be configured in Admin → Settings → Email. If no SMTP settings are configured, the system will attempt to use PHP's mail() function, which may not work on all hosting environments.

Warning

Always test email delivery with a single test message before sending to your full membership.

docs.title: Contact Directory
docs.text: The contact directory lists members with their contact details, grouped by section.

docs.title: Accessing the directory
docs.text: Go to Directory → Contacts. You can filter by section or search by name.

docs.title: Privacy
docs.text: The directory only shows contact details that members have provided and that your administrator has configured as visible. Medical information and other sensitive fields are never shown in the directory.

docs.title: Exporting
docs.text: If you have export permission, a Download CSV button is available at the top of the directory. The export respects the same field visibility rules as the on-screen view.

docs.title: Organogram
docs.text: The organogram is a visual diagram of your organisation's hierarchy.

docs.title: Viewing the organogram
docs.text:
Go to Directory → Organogram to see your full organisation tree. Each node shows the name and type.

Click on a node to see its details and the members assigned to it.

docs.title: Printing
docs.text: Use your browser's print function (Ctrl+P / Cmd+P) to print the organogram. The page is formatted for print output.

docs.title: Calendar
docs.text: The calendar view shows all upcoming events in a monthly or list layout.

docs.title: Switching views
docs.text:
Use the view toggle (top right of the calendar) to switch between:

  • Month — traditional grid calendar
  • List — a chronological list of upcoming events

docs.title: Navigating
docs.text: Use the arrow buttons to move between months. Click Today to return to the current month.

docs.title: Clicking an event
docs.text: Click any event to see its full details, including location and which sections it applies to.

docs.title: iCal Feed
docs.text: ScoutKeeper provides an iCal feed so members can subscribe to the event calendar in their preferred calendar app (Google Calendar, Apple Calendar, Outlook, etc.).

docs.title: Getting the feed URL
docs.text: The iCal feed URL is shown in Events → Calendar → Subscribe. Copy the URL.

docs.title: Subscribing in common apps
docs.text:
Google CalendarApple CalendarOutlook
  1. Open Google Calendar
  2. Click + next to Other calendars
  3. Choose From URL
  4. Paste the ScoutKeeper iCal URL and click Add calendar
  1. Open Calendar
  2. Go to File → New Calendar Subscription
  3. Paste the URL and click Subscribe
  1. Open Outlook Calendar
  2. Go to Add calendar → Subscribe from web
  3. Paste the URL and click Import

Note

The feed is public (no login required) so the URL should be treated as semi-private. Regenerating the URL will break existing subscriptions.

docs.title: Managing Events
docs.text: The Events module provides a calendar for planning and communicating activities.

docs.title: Creating an event
docs.text:
1. Go to Events → Add Event
  2. Enter the event name, date/time, location, and description
  3. Choose which sections the event is relevant to
  4. Save

docs.title: Editing and deleting
docs.text: Open any event from the calendar or list view to edit or delete it.

docs.title: Visibility
docs.text: Events are visible to all logged-in users by default. Future versions will add per-section visibility controls.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Calendar
  • iCal Feed

docs.title: The interface
docs.text:
After logging in you will see the main dashboard. The layout has two parts:

  • Sidebar (left) — navigation grouped by module
  • Topbar (top) — search, your account menu, and dark/light mode toggle

docs.title: 1. Set up your organisation structure
docs.text:
Before adding members, define your group hierarchy under Organisation Structure. This lets you assign members to the right section.

See Organisation Structure.

docs.title: 2. Configure roles and permissions
docs.text:
Review the default roles under Admin → Roles & Permissions. Create roles that match your organisation's staff positions and assign appropriate access.

See Roles & Permissions.

docs.title: 3. Add members
docs.text:
Once your structure is in place, start adding members under Members → Add Member, or import a batch via Members → Bulk Import.

See Managing Members.

docs.title: 4. Create user logins (optional)
docs.text:
If other staff members will use the system, create login accounts for them under Admin → Users & Logins.

See Users & Logins.

docs.title: 1. Download ScoutKeeper
docs.text: Download the latest release from the GitHub releases page. Get the .zip file — not the source code archive.

docs.title: 2. Create a database
docs.text:
In your hosting control panel (cPanel, Plesk, etc.):

  1. Create a new MySQL database (e.g. scoutkeeper)
  2. Create a database user with a strong password
  3. Grant the user all privileges on the new database
  4. Note down the database name, username, password, and host (usually localhost)

docs.title: 3. Upload the files
docs.text:
Extract the zip and upload all files to your web server. You can upload to:

  • The root of your domain (public_html/) if ScoutKeeper is your main site
  • A subdirectory (e.g. public_html/scouts/) if it will run alongside other sites

Tip

Upload using FTP or your hosting file manager. The upload may take a few minutes due to the number of files.

docs.title: 4. Run the setup wizard
docs.text:
Open your browser and navigate to the URL where you uploaded the files:

  • Root install: https://yourdomain.org
  • Subdirectory install: https://yourdomain.org/scouts

You will be redirected automatically to the setup wizard. Continue to Setup Wizard.

docs.title: Getting Started Overview
docs.text: ScoutKeeper is installed on your own web hosting. There is no cloud subscription — your data stays on your server.

docs.title: What you need
docs.text:
• A Linux web hosting account with PHP 8.2+ and MySQL 8.0+
  • The ability to upload files (FTP or cPanel file manager)
  • Around 15 minutes for the initial setup

docs.title: The setup process
docs.text:
1. Check requirements — confirm your hosting meets the minimum spec
  2. Install — upload the files and create the database
  3. Run the setup wizard — configure your organisation through a browser
  4. First login — explore the interface and add your first members

docs.title: After setup
docs.text:
Once your site is running, the main areas you will use are:

  • Members — adding, editing, and searching member records
  • Organisation Structure — setting up your group hierarchy
  • Events — managing your calendar
  • Admin — managing user accounts and system settings

docs.title: Server requirements
docs.text:
Component Minimum Recommended PHP 8.2 8.3 MySQL 8.0 8.0+ Disk space 200 MB 1 GB+ PHP extensions pdo_mysql, mbstring, json, openssl, fileinfo —

Most standard Linux shared hosting plans (cPanel, Plesk, DirectAdmin) meet these requirements.

Windows hosting

ScoutKeeper is tested on Linux. Windows hosting is not supported.

docs.title: PHP extensions
docs.text: The setup wizard will check for required extensions and warn you if anything is missing. If you need to enable an extension, contact your hosting provider.

docs.title: Browser requirements (for administrators)
docs.text:
ScoutKeeper works in all modern browsers. Internet Explorer is not supported.

  • Chrome 90+
  • Firefox 90+
  • Safari 15+
  • Edge 90+

docs.title: Setup Wizard
docs.text: The setup wizard runs automatically on first visit. It walks you through configuring ScoutKeeper in your browser — no command line needed.

docs.title: Step 1: System check
docs.text: The wizard checks that your server meets the requirements. Any failed checks are shown with guidance on how to fix them. You cannot proceed until all required checks pass.

docs.title: Step 2: Database connection
docs.text:
Enter your database credentials:

  • Host — usually localhost
  • Database name — the database you created
  • Username and Password — the database user you created

The wizard will test the connection before continuing.

docs.title: Step 3: Organisation details
docs.text: Enter your organisation's name and select your country. These values can be changed later in Admin → Settings.

docs.title: Step 4: Administrator account
docs.text:
Create the first administrator login:

  • Enter a name, email address, and a strong password
  • This account has full access to all features
  • Additional accounts can be added later in Admin → Users & Logins

docs.title: Step 5: Complete
docs.text:
The wizard creates the database tables and writes your configuration file. When it finishes, click Go to ScoutKeeper to log in for the first time.

Info

The wizard creates a config/config.php file on your server. Keep this file private — it contains your database credentials.

docs.title: Adding Members
docs.text: Go to Members → Add Member to create a new member record.

docs.title: Required fields
docs.text:
• First name and last name
  • Date of birth
  • Section assignment (requires organisation structure to be set up first)

docs.title: Optional fields
docs.text:
• Contact details (email, phone, address)
  • Emergency contact
  • Medical information (stored encrypted)
  • Any custom fields your administrator has configured

docs.title: Saving the record
docs.text:
Click Save Member to create the record. The member will appear immediately in the member list.

Tip

To add many members at once, use Bulk Import instead.

docs.title: Bulk Import
docs.text: Bulk import lets you add many members at once from a CSV file.

docs.title: Preparing your CSV
docs.text:
Your CSV file must have a header row. Required columns:

  • first_name
  • last_name
  • date_of_birth (format: YYYY-MM-DD)

Optional columns: email, phone, section (must match an existing section name).

Download the template CSV from Members → Bulk Import for the correct format.

docs.title: Running the import
docs.text:
1. Go to Members → Bulk Import
  2. Select your CSV file
  3. Map the columns if the wizard cannot auto-detect them
  4. Review the preview — rows with errors are highlighted
  5. Click Import to create the records

docs.title: After import
docs.text: A summary shows how many records were created and any rows that were skipped. Skipped rows can be downloaded as a CSV for correction and re-import.

docs.title: Custom Fields
docs.text:
Custom fields let you capture additional information on member records that is specific to your organisation.

Custom fields are configured by an administrator under Admin → Settings → Custom Fields.

docs.title: Field types
docs.text: Type Use for Text Short free-text values Textarea Longer notes Date Date values Dropdown A fixed list of options Checkbox Yes/No values

docs.title: Managing custom fields
docs.text:
1. Go to Admin → Settings → Custom Fields
  2. Click Add Field
  3. Enter a label, choose a type, and mark as required if needed
  4. Save — the field appears on all member records immediately

Reorder fields by dragging the handle on the left. Fields can be hidden but not deleted once data has been entered against them.

docs.title: Managing Members
docs.text: The Members module is the core of ScoutKeeper. It stores all member records for your organisation.

docs.title: Member list
docs.text:
Navigate to Members in the sidebar to see the full member list. You can:

  • Search by name, email, or membership number
  • Filter by section, status, or custom fields
  • Sort by any column

docs.title: Member record
docs.text:
Each member has:

  • Basic details — name, date of birth, contact information
  • Membership details — join date, section assignment, membership status
  • Custom fields — any additional fields your organisation has configured
  • Timeline — a log of events and notes on the member's record
  • Attachments — uploaded documents (e.g. consent forms)
  • Achievements — badges and training records

docs.title: Member statuses
docs.text: Status Meaning Active Current member Inactive Former member, record retained Waiting On the waiting list

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Adding Members
  • Bulk Import
  • Custom Fields
  • Waiting List

docs.title: Member Registration
docs.text: ScoutKeeper can accept public registration requests, allowing families to register interest online without needing an account.

docs.title: Enabling registration
docs.text:
Go to Admin → Settings and enable Public Registration. You can configure:

  • Which sections are open for registration
  • A message shown to applicants
  • Whether to require Terms & Conditions acceptance

docs.title: The registration form
docs.text: Registrants fill in basic details and select a section. On submission, the entry is added to the Waiting List for staff to review.

docs.title: Registration link
docs.text:
Once enabled, the public registration URL is shown in Admin → Settings. Share this link on your website or social media.

Note

Registration does not create a user login. It only adds an entry to the waiting list.

docs.title: Waiting List
docs.text: The waiting list holds prospective members who have expressed interest but cannot yet be accommodated.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text: Waiting list entries are a lightweight record containing contact details and the section applied for. They do not count as full member records and do not appear in member reports.

docs.title: Adding to the waiting list
docs.text: Go to Members → Waiting List → Add Entry. Enter the applicant's name, contact details, and the section they are interested in.

docs.title: Converting to a member
docs.text:
When a place becomes available:

  1. Open the waiting list entry
  2. Click Convert to Member
  3. The form pre-fills with the existing details — complete any missing fields and save

The waiting list entry is removed once conversion is confirmed.

docs.title: Public registration
docs.text: If your administrator has enabled public registration, prospective members can add themselves to the waiting list via a public URL. See Member Registration.

docs.title: Adding a node
docs.text:
1. Go to Organisation Structure
  2. Click Add Node
  3. Enter the name, choose the type, and select the parent node
  4. Save

docs.title: Node types
docs.text: Node types are defined by your administrator in Admin → Settings → Level Types. Common examples: District, Group, Section, Unit.

docs.title: Moving a node
docs.text: To move a node to a different parent, open the node and change the parent field. All child nodes move with it.

docs.title: Deleting a node
docs.text: A node can only be deleted if it has no members assigned and no child nodes. Deactivate the node instead if you want to retain the history.

docs.title: Organisation Structure Overview
docs.text: ScoutKeeper models your Scout organisation as a hierarchy of nodes — districts, groups, sections, and any other levels your organisation uses.

docs.title: Concepts
docs.text:
Nodes are the building blocks of the hierarchy. Each node has:

  • A name and type (e.g. Group, Section, District)
  • A parent node (except the root)
  • Optional contact information

Teams are cross-cutting groups that do not follow the hierarchy — useful for leadership teams or sub-committees.

docs.title: Setting up your hierarchy
docs.text:
Before adding members, create the hierarchy that reflects your organisation:

  1. Go to Organisation Structure
  2. Add your top-level node (e.g. the Group name)
  3. Add child nodes for each section (e.g. Beavers, Cubs, Scouts)

Members are then assigned to a section node when their record is created.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Groups & Sections
  • Teams

docs.title: Teams
docs.text: Teams are used for groups that cut across the normal hierarchy — for example, a leadership team or a committee that includes members from multiple sections.

docs.title: Creating a team
docs.text:
1. Go to Organisation Structure → Teams
  2. Click Add Team
  3. Enter a name and optionally assign a team leader
  4. Save

docs.title: Adding members to a team
docs.text: Open the team and use the Add Member button to search for and add existing members. A member can belong to multiple teams.

docs.title: Teams vs. sections
docs.text: Section Team Appears in hierarchy Yes No Members assigned at registration Yes No Used for access scoping Yes Yes

docs.title: Changelog
docs.text: See the GitHub releases page for the full changelog.

docs.title: Before 2004 — The Spreadsheet Era
docs.text: The earliest version of what would become ScoutKeeper was not a system at all — it was a collection of spreadsheets and local databases used to manage a single scout troop.

docs.title: 2004 — Beginnings
docs.text: It became clear that a web-based platform could serve the troop better, even with only around 60 members. Work began on a simple online system that allowed parents, scouts, and leaders to interact with the same platform. Web technologies in 2004 were limited compared to today, but the idea of a role-based, multi-level membership system was already taking shape.

docs.title: 2006 — Concept
docs.text: The idea of expanding into a national scouting information portal was pitched — and shot down. Paper remained the primary way to disseminate and control information across the organisation.

docs.title: 2007 — Launch
docs.text: ScoutKeeper was launched as an independent information portal on a personal initiative. The project was initially met with scepticism, with many assuming it would fizzle out within a few months.

docs.title: 2008 — Momentum
docs.text: Within 12 months of launch, more than half the Scout Groups in the Association were using the system regularly. New features to assist the management of groups were steadily added.

docs.title: 2009 — Official Platform
docs.text:
The National Scout Council adopted ScoutKeeper as The Scout Association of Malta's official membership platform. The system served an organisation of around 3,000 members and handled membership records, training records, youth progress tracking, statistics, and a range of other day-to-day operational functions. For a period, ScoutKeeper also served as the booking engine for the national campsite — functionality that was eventually separated into a dedicated system, today known as campsite.mt.

Over the following years, the system went through several revisions with features being added continuously.

docs.title: 2014 — Incorporation
docs.text: QuadNine Ltd was incorporated in 2014. Ownership of ScoutKeeper was transferred to QuadNine, ending more than seven years of private funding for the system's hosting, maintenance, and upgrades. TSAM began paying a token licence fee to partly cover the costs of running the platform.

docs.title: 2020 — End of Life
docs.text: By 2020, the system was built on technology approaching fifteen years old. Keeping pace with modern browsers and the expectations of members had become increasingly difficult. It was decided that ScoutKeeper had reached the end of its useful life, and TSAM would begin looking for a replacement.

docs.title: 2022 — Migration
docs.text: TSAM adopted a new commercial membership platform and migrated its data from ScoutKeeper. The cost of the commercial system prompted some reflection on whether a capable, open-source alternative could fill that gap for organisations that cannot justify similar fees. At the time, it remained an idea rather than a plan.

docs.title: 2026 — ScoutKeeper 10
docs.text:
Over time, Kevin's involvement in the wider Scout movement gave him a clearer picture of the challenges facing smaller organisations. Many lack the resources or technical expertise to build or commission a proper membership system, leaving them to manage their affairs with spreadsheets and workarounds — the same starting point ScoutKeeper itself came from two decades earlier. Larger, better-funded associations have no such problem. The gap is real, and it shows.

Making ScoutKeeper 10 open source was not an obvious decision. It means forgoing potential revenue. But the alternative — a commercial product that prices out the organisations that need it most — felt like the wrong answer. An open-source platform that any organisation can copy, install, and use with minimal cost or technical knowledge creates a more level playing field.

The vision behind ScoutKeeper 10 has two sides. The first is practical: a Scout organisation exists to deliver scouting to young people, and it should not have to spend precious volunteer time and limited funds on administration that a well-built, freely available system could handle. Better tools mean better governance, and better governance means more energy left for the things that actually matter.

The second is less direct. The Scout movement has well-established policies and recommended practices for how organisations should be run — but translating those into day-to-day reality can be daunting, particularly for smaller groups without dedicated administrative support. ScoutKeeper 10 is designed with those processes built in, making it easier to simply do things the right way. For organisations that choose not to use the system at all, it can still serve as a working reference — a concrete example of how good governance practices can be implemented in practice, not just described in a policy document.

Development began in earnest at the start of 2026. ScoutKeeper 10 is a personal project by Kevin Camilleri, funded through QuadNine.

docs.title: Licence
docs.text:
ScoutKeeper is released under the GNU Affero General Public License v3.0 (AGPL-3.0).

You are free to use, modify, and distribute ScoutKeeper under the terms of that licence. If you run a modified version as a network service, you must make the modified source code available.

See the full licence text in the repository: LICENSE.
